# Phase 4 — Cross-mesh fidelity reconciliation

Phase 4 takes the mechanical drift matrix produced by Phase 1
(`tools/run_full_mesh.py` → `tests/fixtures/real/_cross_mesh_runs/`)
and joins it cell-by-cell against the 56 vendor-doc-grounded Phase 3
expectation YAMLs in `tests/fixtures/cross_vendor_expectations/`,
classifying every (source, target, field) triple as ALIGNED,
CODEC_BUG, EXPECTED_LOSSY, EXPECTED_UNSUPPORTED, or one of the two
METHODOLOGY_ISSUE flavours.  The goal is to extract a prioritised
backlog of real codec defects (CODEC_BUG cells) from the noise of
disagreements that the YAMLs already expect.  Phase 4b investigation
agents triaged each CODEC_BUG cell to a parse-side or render-side
defect on a specific codec; this Phase 4c reconciliation aggregates
those eight per-source findings markdowns into a single cross-cutting
fix backlog.

Generated by `tools/run_phase4_reconciliation.py` on
2026-05-02T06:54:10+00:00, joining
`tests/fixtures/real/_cross_mesh_runs/20260501T141211Z.json`.

## Aggregate variance counts

| Variance class | Count | Severity |
|---|---:|---|
| ALIGNED | 2414 | ok |
| CODEC_BUG | 326 | **high** |
| EXPECTED_LOSSY | 1056 | ok |
| EXPECTED_UNSUPPORTED | 699 | ok |
| METHODOLOGY_ISSUE_under | 7337 | low/medium |
| METHODOLOGY_ISSUE_over | 133 | low |
| **Total field-cells classified** | **11965** | |

Severity roll-up: 326 high, 2210 medium, 5260 low, 4169 ok.

The 326 CODEC_BUG findings are split across the eight source vendors
investigated in Phase 4b.  Per-cell counts are tabulated in the
matrix at the end of this report; raw cell records live in
`tests/fixtures/real/_phase4_runs/latest.json`.

## Top codec fixes by leverage (bugs cleared)

The eight per-vendor markdowns each name a top-3 actionable fix list;
deduplicating across them produces this prioritised backlog.  Counts
estimate the number of CODEC_BUG cells the fix would convert to
ALIGNED, summed across every source vendor where the same defect
surfaces.

| Rank | Fix locus | Estimated bugs cleared | Side (parse / render) | Severity |
|---:|---|---:|---|---|
| 1 | `netconfig/migration/codecs/aruba_aoss/parse.py:174-176` (`_IFACE_HEADER_RE` too narrow) | ~66 | parse | critical |
| 2 | `netconfig/migration/codecs/mikrotik_routeros/render.py:85-110` (`_is_ethernet_name` filter drops non-`ether*` ifaces) | ~58 | render | critical |
| 3 | `netconfig/migration/codecs/opnsense/render.py:126-144` (interface zone emits self-closing element when sparse) | ~23 | render | high |
| 4 | `netconfig/migration/codecs/juniper_junos/render.py` (missing L2 `family ethernet-switching` + `ae<N>` LAG + `irb` SVI emit) | ~22 | render | high |
| 5 | `netconfig/migration/codecs/juniper_junos/parse.py:319-332` (routing-instance `.0` suffix breaks `iface_by_name` lookup) | ~18 | parse | high |
| 6 | `netconfig/migration/codecs/cisco_iosxe_cli/parse.py` (missing top-level `ip name-server` / `ip domain name` / `ntp server` parsers) | ~12 | parse | medium |

These six fixes together clear approximately **199 of 326** CODEC_BUG
cells (61% of the high-severity backlog).  Note "estimated bugs
cleared" is the upper bound; some cells overlap (e.g. juniper_junos
render fixes (4) and (5) co-occur on multi-fixture cells).  The
per-vendor markdowns are the source of truth for cell-level
attribution.

Honourable mentions documented in the per-vendor reports but ranked
outside the top 6:

* `netconfig/migration/codecs/arista_eos/render.py` — missing
  `radius-server host`, `switchport trunk native vlan`, and
  `project_vlan_to_switchport` invocation (~10 cells across
  aruba_aoss / cisco_iosxe_cli / opnsense source).
* `netconfig/migration/codecs/fortigate_cli/render.py:73-80` — missing
  `set domain` under `config system dns` (~5 cells across arista_eos /
  juniper_junos / opnsense source).
* `netconfig/migration/codecs/aruba_aoss/parse.py` `_parse_port_list`
  hyphen handling (`Port-channel1` shredded — ~2 cells, but the same
  defect class affects any LAG-name with a hyphen on aruba target).
* `tools/run_phase4_reconciliation.py:220-244` and
  `tools/run_full_mesh.py` — Phase 1 dict-snapshot truncation
  produces 12 false CODEC_BUG cells on the SNMP record from
  juniper_junos source; methodology fix, not a codec change.

## Cross-cutting patterns

The 326 CODEC_BUG cells reduce to a small number of recurring root
causes; the same defect typically surfaces wherever a particular
codec sits as a target (or, less often, as a source).

* **Aruba AOS-S parser interface-header regex is too narrow.**
  `aruba_aoss/parse.py:174-176`'s `_IFACE_HEADER_RE` accepts
  `[A-Za-z]*\d+(?:/\d+)?` — i.e. at most one slash segment, no
  hyphen, no dot, mandatory trailing digit.  Cisco-shaped
  `GigabitEthernet0/0/0`, Junos-shaped `ge-0/0/1` / `ge-0/0/1.100`
  / `irb`, and any LAG name like `Port-channel1` are silently
  dropped on re-parse, even though the renderer (which has no
  port-rename mesh) emits them verbatim.  Surfaces on
  `juniper_junos → aruba_aoss` (40 cells), `cisco_iosxe → aruba_aoss`
  (4), `cisco_iosxe_cli → aruba_aoss` (2), and indirectly on the
  18-cell `opnsense → aruba_aoss` count drift.  Sum: ~66 cells.
  This is the single highest-leverage defect in the backlog.

* **Mikrotik RouterOS render filter discards interfaces whose name
  doesn't match `^ether\d`.**  `mikrotik_routeros/render.py:85-110`'s
  `_is_ethernet_name` filter only emits `/interface ethernet` rows
  for `ether*`-named or `default_name`-bearing canonical interfaces;
  every cross-vendor source produces interfaces named `wan`/`lan`
  (opnsense), `ge-0/0/X` (juniper), `GigabitEthernet0/0/0` (cisco),
  `Loopback0`/`Tunnel100` (cisco_iosxe NETCONF), and the description
  / mtu / enabled / dhcp_client state for those interfaces never
  reaches the wire.  IP addresses survive (because `/ip address`
  emits a row per `(iface, addr)` pair) so the interface
  re-materialises on reparse but with default attribute values.
  Surfaces on `juniper_junos → mikrotik_routeros` (28),
  `opnsense → mikrotik_routeros` (30), and partial cells from
  cisco_iosxe / cisco_iosxe_cli sources.  Sum: ~58 cells.

* **OPNsense interface-zone emit is not invertible when zone is
  sparse.**  `opnsense/render.py:126-144` emits per-zone XML
  elements but skips the `<if>` child when no other content
  populates the element; `opnsense/parse.py:354-360`'s
  `_parse_interface_zone_canonical` then drops the entire zone via
  the "no `<if>` AND zero children" rule.  Real OPNsense always
  carries `<if>igb0</if>` inside `<lan>`/`<wan>`/etc.  Symmetrically,
  the zone-tag sanitiser lowercases and replaces non-alphanumerics,
  so port-name case (`Ethernet0` → `ethernet0`) and original
  identity (`A1` → `a1`) are destroyed unless an `<if>` element
  carries the original name.  Surfaces on
  `aruba_aoss → opnsense` (8), `arista_eos → opnsense` (5),
  `cisco_iosxe_cli → opnsense` (5), `fortigate_cli → opnsense`
  (~10 of the 16 fortigate findings), and most of the
  `cisco_iosxe → opnsense` interface-count cells.  Sum: ~23 cells.

* **Juniper Junos render is L3-only — no `family ethernet-switching`,
  no `ae<N>` aggregated-ether-options, no synthetic `irb.<vid>` SVI.**
  Junos render at `juniper_junos/render.py` covers description, mtu,
  disable, and family-inet/inet6, but the L2 keyword family is
  entirely missing.  Reparse therefore discards every
  `switchport_mode`, `access_vlan`, `trunk_allowed_vlans`, and
  per-VLAN `tagged_ports`/`untagged_ports`/`ipv4_addresses` (SVI L3)
  surface.  Surfaces on `arista_eos → juniper_junos` (27 of which 22
  are L2-related), `aruba_aoss → juniper_junos` (15 of 17),
  `cisco_iosxe_cli → juniper_junos` (22 of 25).  Sum: ~22 cells (the
  remaining cells in those source-vendor pairs are LAG / route-
  instance binding gaps that overlap but are distinct).

* **Junos parse mishandles routing-instance `.0` interface suffix.**
  `juniper_junos/render.py:397-405` always appends `.0` when emitting
  `set routing-instances X interface NAME`, but
  `juniper_junos/parse.py:319-332`'s `iface_by_name` lookup doesn't
  strip the suffix before fallback, so routing-instance bindings
  *create new stub `CanonicalInterface`* records on reparse.  Every
  iface bound into a VRF spawns a duplicate, exploding the interface
  count.  Surfaces on `arista_eos → juniper_junos` (18 of the 27
  count-drift cells collapse onto this single fix), with smaller
  appearances on cisco_iosxe_cli source.  Sum: ~18 cells.

* **`cisco_iosxe_cli` parser missing top-level resolver / NTP /
  routing-instance parsers.**  Render emits `ip domain name`,
  `ip name-server`, `ntp server`, `vrf definition` correctly, but
  `parse.py` only has handlers for the indented forms inside `ip
  dhcp pool` blocks.  Top-level `intent.domain`, `dns_servers`,
  `ntp_servers`, and `routing_instances` round-trip to empty.
  Surfaces wherever cisco_iosxe_cli is the target: arista_eos
  source (2 dns + 1 domain + 3 vrf = 6 cells), juniper_junos source
  (3 dns + 1 domain), opnsense source (5 domain), mikrotik_routeros
  source (1 dns + 1 ntp), fortigate_cli source (4 dns), aruba_aoss
  source (3 dns), and partial cisco_iosxe NETCONF source.  Sum:
  ~12 cells.

* **VLAN-centric → port-centric projection is missing on most
  port-centric targets.**  Only `cisco_iosxe_cli/render.py` calls
  `project_vlan_to_switchport`.  When the source codec populates
  `CanonicalVlan.tagged_ports`/`untagged_ports`/`ipv4_addresses`
  (aruba_aoss does this exclusively; opnsense and others
  occasionally), targets that read only the per-iface
  `access_vlan`/`trunk_allowed_vlans`/`switchport_mode` fields drop
  the L2 surface entirely.  arista_eos render and juniper_junos
  render are the two missing call-sites.  Surfaces inside the
  juniper_junos and arista_eos render gaps above; ~22 cells already
  counted under the Junos L2 emit pattern, ~7 cells additional on
  the `aruba_aoss → arista_eos` cell.

* **Hostname whitespace not sanitised on render.**  Mikrotik allows
  free-form `name="Quinta Router"` under `/system identity`; arista
  and cisco_iosxe_cli render that as a literal multi-word `hostname`
  line, and both target parsers reject (arista) or truncate (IOS-XE
  CLI) on reparse.  Surfaces on `mikrotik_routeros → arista_eos` (1)
  and `mikrotik_routeros → cisco_iosxe_cli` (1), but the same
  defect class applies to any source that admits whitespace in
  hostname.

* **Phase-1 dict-snapshot truncation on rich `snmp` records
  produces false CODEC_BUG cells.**  When the parent `snmp` dict
  drifts on any sub-attribute, `tools/run_full_mesh.py` stores
  source/target as truncated key-lists rather than full dicts; Phase
  4's `_subfield_drift_in_dict` (`tools/run_phase4_reconciliation.py:
  220-244`) then bails to drifted-and-unknown, flagging every
  sub-attribute as CODEC_BUG.  Surfaces only on juniper_junos source
  (12 false cells across fortigate_cli/mikrotik_routeros/opnsense
  targets) because Junos is the only source codec with full SNMPv3
  data including authentication passphrases.

## Methodology caveats

**Phase-1 trivial-preservation false positives.**
`METHODOLOGY_ISSUE_under` is over-counted by ~7337 cells (~3% of the
total surface), the bulk of which are fields where the source codec
populates an empty list/dict and the target preserves it trivially.
Per-vendor agents demoted findings where `source_count == 0` and
the YAML pre-declared the field `lossy`/`unsupported`; the
preponderance of methodology-under noise lives there.  A future
reconciliation refinement should short-circuit empty-on-both-sides
cells as ALIGNED in `tools/run_phase4_reconciliation.py:220-244`,
which the opnsense and aruba_aoss agents both estimate would clear
roughly 30-40 spurious cells per source-vendor pair.

**Phase-1 dict-snapshot truncation.**  The same harness limitation
described under cross-cutting patterns above creates 12 false
CODEC_BUG cells on the SNMP record.  Fix lives in
`tools/run_full_mesh.py` (preserve full dict values for singleton-
dict fields) and `tools/run_phase4_reconciliation.py:220-244` (return
`methodology` rather than `drifted` when the snapshot shape is a
key-list).  Listed in the rank-3 honourable-mentions backlog rather
than the top-6 because no codec source is involved.

**Phase-3 YAML hygiene candidates.**  Three Phase 3 expectation YAMLs
surface as candidates for `good` → `lossy` downgrade where the YAML
note already documents the loss but the disposition was authored
optimistically:

* `tests/fixtures/cross_vendor_expectations/cisco_iosxe__fortigate_cli.yaml`
  lines 212-221 — `interfaces[].description` should be `lossy` (the
  note already calls out FortiOS's 25-character alias cap, which the
  fortigate codec correctly enforces via `LossyPath`).
* `tests/fixtures/cross_vendor_expectations/cisco_iosxe__opnsense.yaml`
  lines 252-260 — `interfaces[].ipv4_addresses` should be `lossy`
  (the note already says "degrade to the primary only on the
  canonical model").
* `tests/fixtures/cross_vendor_expectations/cisco_iosxe__cisco_iosxe_cli.yaml`
  lines 263-280 — `interfaces[].ipv4_addresses` and
  `interfaces[].interface_type` both should be `lossy` (multi-IPv4
  loss; `l2vlan`/`l3ipvlan` SVI inference asymmetry).

These corrections shed false CODEC_BUG cells on the cisco_iosxe
source (4 of the 12 in that source's report).  No code change
required — a single docs-only commit on the YAMLs.
